figures.caption: Figure 4: Trajectory length by task difficulty for all 19 agents. Every agent takes more steps on harder tasks (all Spearman 𝜌 < 0, all 𝑝 < 0.01). This universal length–difficulty coupling explains the confounding reversal: length reflects task difficulty, not strategy quality.

Table 6: Length–failure effect. Approach A (top): for each agent, mean steps of resolved vs. failed runs across all tasks; every agent shows failures are significantly longer (𝑝 < 0.001, one-sided Mann-Whitney𝑈 ). Approach B (bottom): for each contested task, mean steps of resolved vs. failed agents; the effect reverses: resolved trajectories are longer on 63% of tasks (𝑝 < 10−8, two-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank).

Figure 1: Contrasting agent trajectories on django-15863. Both agents use the SWE-agent framework. Claude 4 Sonnet (left) follows a structured workflow: browse the codebase, reproduce the bug, make one surgical edit, then run several verification cycles before submitting. GPT-4 (right) finds the correct file but then edits the code many times, producing 28 syntax errors, without ever running the project’s test suite.

Figure 5: Same task and length (32 steps), different agent, different outcome. Left: CodeSweep/kimi-k2 explores, struggles, breaks through (Vp), submits. Right: SWE-agent/gpt-4 skips exploration, enters an endless P→Ve spiral, never recovers.

Figure 10: System prompt comparison for claude-4-sonnet on SWE-agent (top) vs. OpenHands (bottom). Quoted text extracted verbatim from trajectory files. Despite a 16× difference in instruction length (350 vs. 5,602 characters), the LLM produces near-identical core behavioral metrics on both frameworks.
Table 1: Enriched encoding: 13 sub-phase symbols. Each symbol is classified from the raw .traj data using deterministic regex rules on the action and observation fields.

Figure 6: Three structural dimensions of RQ2b across all agents. (a) Context gathering: agents that delay their first edit succeed more (𝜌 = +0.68, 𝑝 < 0.001). (b) Opening patch intensity: agents that spend more of their first 10 steps patching succeed less (𝜌 = −0.78, 𝑝 < 0.001). (c) Validation effort: agents that spend more of their trajectory on validation succeed more (𝜌 = +0.50, 𝑝 < 0.05)

Figure 7: Strategy stability across task complexity for 6 representative agents (3 SWE-agent in red, 3 OpenHands in blue). (a) Context gathering, (b) opening patch intensity, and (c) validation effort are plotted against patch complexity (lines changed).

Table 3: Twelve never-solved tasks with simple patches. The Human label column shows the official difficulty annotation from SWE-bench Verified’s human annotators [20]. By every prior difficulty metric and by human judgment, these are easy tasks. Zero of 19 agents solve any of them.
Figure 8: Resolution rate for 19 agents on SWE-benchVerified (500 tasks), colored by framework.
Table 5: Architectural gap across all 12 never-solved simplepatch tasks. The best agent found the gold-patch file in 12/12 and edited it in 10/12, yet failed every time.

Figure 3: Representative case study: matplotlib-23476. The agent fixes DPI scaling in display backends (4 files); the gold patch fixes the serialization in __getstate__ (3 lines). Both address the same bug from different architectural levels.
